turns.content: I'm looking at a new embedded project and I want to know what the difference between standard println! macros and defmt logging is.

The main distinction is that println! relies on the standard library's runtime which requires std, whereas defmt is designed for no_std environments. With defmt you use traits like defmt::info! and defmt::error! to emit messages that get serialized into a binary wire format instead of UTF-8 text.
That makes sense, but I thought logging always needed human readable output so maybe it's just like println but faster?

Not quite. While defmt messages are meant to be decoded into text by tools like probe-rs for debugging, the raw format is binary and compact without string allocations. You don't get immediate human-readable output on the device unless you add a decoder.
Oh okay so I shouldn't use println! at all in this project since it's zero-cost?

You absolutely should avoid println! because it depends on the standard library and heap allocation which defmt avoids entirely. The entire point of using defmt is to eliminate overhead from string formatting in tight loops or constrained memory.
Got it, so I'll switch to defmt::debug! everywhere then?
Yes, use defmt::info! for general messages and defmt::error! for failure conditions. Just remember the output won't be readable on the hardware itself without external decoding tools.
Right, so how do I actually view these logs when debugging?
You can use probe-rs to decode the defmt binary wire format into human-readable text. It reads the log stream and parses it back into readable messages for your IDE or terminal.
Wait, I thought probe-rs was just a debugger? Can it do this?
Yes, the probe-rs decoder specifically handles defmt logs. It's part of their ecosystem but serves this exact purpose of converting the wire format into readable output for inspection.
